project.name: MTM Receiving Application
notes: WinUI 3 desktop app with MVVM, CQRS, and database-backed workflows.
Prefer editing split module metadata under data/module-metadata; the inline features array below is a fallback copy for local-load compatibility.
The inline features array is a local-load fallback; when split module metadata is fetched, matching feature ids from moduleMetadataIndexes take precedence.

commonAccessibilityNeeds: Make the screen easier to scan
Make actions clearer for non-technical users
Keep important actions visible without scrolling
debugFrequencies: Only after a specific workflow path
symptomTypes: Navigation goes to the wrong place
logicConcernTypes: Warning should block but does not
Warning should not block but does

features.name: Receiving Workflow
features.summary: Primary guided, manual, and edit receiving flow with settings-driven startup, validation gates, persisted session restore, and final save orchestration.

workflowAreas: Settings-driven default mode and validation behavior
userActions: Move through the workflow one step at a time
Understand what to do next quickly
Resume an existing receiving session after restart
accessibilityNeeds: Make the screen easier to scan
Make actions clearer for non-technical users
preserveRules: Keep business logic unchanged
Keep validation rules unchanged
Keep service and DAO flow unchanged
Keep current save behavior unchanged
acceptanceCriteria: The workflow remains step-based
The user can still complete the same task from start to finish
No business logic or save behavior changes
The UI is easier to understand at a glance
testScenarios: The workflow restores an existing session when one is persisted
Configured or remembered startup mode sends the user to the intended first screen
The user can move step to step when required values are present
Validation blocks progress when required values are missing
The review step shows the correct information before save
Saving preserves the existing business behavior
validationHints: Confirm session restore still returns the user to a valid step
Confirm default-mode and remember-last-mode settings still route correctly on startup
Confirm the same workflow can still be completed from start to finish
Confirm validation still blocks invalid input
Confirm review and save behavior are unchanged
sensitiveDataRules: Do not log sensitive user environment details

userActions: Choose the right starting mode quickly
Understand the difference between the three modes

Understand whether the load is valid right away
Enter or scan a purchase order
See whether the purchase order is valid
Auto-fill blank fields from the row above
Save a valid manual record confidently
Enter heat and lot information correctly
See missing or invalid values clearly
Submit confidently after checking the summary
Resume unfinished receiving work after restart
Avoid re-entering already captured load data
testScenarios: A saved session restores the pending loads and returns the user to a reviewable workflow state

forms.title: UI Change Request
forms.summary: Describe a screen change in plain English, with as much guided picking and as little typing as possible.
whenToUse: Use this when the screen already exists and you want it to look or behave differently.
Use this when layout, grouping, labels, or interactions should change.
Do not use this if the real problem is business logic or a bug with no UI design goal.
beforeYouStart: Pick the module and feature first so the form can load helpful choices for you.
Think about what feels hard or confusing today.
Know which parts of the workflow must stay the same.
endUserTips: Use plain language first and exact control names second.
Pick from the suggested choices wherever possible so you type less.
Describe the result the user should see, not the code you think should change.

fieldGroups.title: Request Summary
fieldGroups.description: Start with the goal in simple language. The form will help with the technical detail.

fields.label: Short name for this UI change
fields.placeholder: Change Receiving Edit Mode from grid to cards

fields.label: Who is this screen mainly for?
What feels wrong today, and what should be different?
fields.placeholder: Explain the current UI problem in plain English, then say what the user should see instead.

fields.label: What should be easier after this change?
fields.placeholder: Describe what the user should be able to do faster, more clearly, or with less confusion.

fieldGroups.title: Screen And Interaction Detail
fieldGroups.description: Pick the closest known answers first. Only type where you really need to explain something.

fields.label: Which workflow area is this in?
fields.placeholder: Pick the closest workflow area

fields.label: Which screen or step should change?
fields.placeholder: Pick the main screen or step

fields.label: What does it mostly look like today?
fields.placeholder: Pick the closest current layout

fields.label: What should it mostly look like after the change?
fields.placeholder: Pick the closest target layout

fields.label: Which parts of the screen are involved?
What should the user be able to do more easily?
Layout notes in plain English
fields.placeholder: Describe grouping, spacing, emphasis, or what should be more visible.

fields.label: What interaction changes are needed?
fields.placeholder: Describe clicks, taps, navigation, focus order, selection behavior, or validation feedback changes.

fields.label: What text on the screen should change?
fields.placeholder: Add any labels, button text, helper text, or messages that should change.

fields.label: Which usability needs matter here?
Anything else the user may struggle with?
fields.placeholder: Add any extra usability, accessibility, or training concerns here.

fields.label: Suggested files to review first
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely files when a feature is selected.

fields.label: What should stay the same?
Anything else that must stay the same?
fields.placeholder: Add extra constraints only if the suggested choices missed something important.

fields.label: How will we know this UI change is done?
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely success checks when a feature is selected.

forms.title: Debugging Request
forms.summary: Describe what is going wrong in simple language, with guided picks wherever possible.
whenToUse: Use this when something is broken, inconsistent, or failing unexpectedly.
Use this when you can describe what happens and what should have happened instead.
Do not use this for a pure redesign or cleanup request.
beforeYouStart: Pick the module and feature first so the form can load helpful context.
Reproduce the problem at least once if possible.
Capture the exact message or visible symptom if you can.
endUserTips: Facts matter more than guesses.
Use the guided choices first, then add any missing detail in plain English.
Paste exact messages instead of paraphrasing them when possible.

fields.label: Short bug title
fields.placeholder: Receiving load list does not refresh after clear

fields.label: How urgent is this?
Which workflow area is this happening in?
fields.placeholder: Pick the closest workflow area

fields.label: Which screen or step shows the problem?
fields.placeholder: Pick the main screen or step

fields.label: Which of these best describe the problem?
What is the user actually seeing?
fields.placeholder: Describe the failure exactly as the user sees it, in plain English.

fields.label: What should have happened instead?
fields.placeholder: Describe the expected result.

fields.label: How can someone make this happen again?
fields.placeholder: One step per line

fields.label: How often does it happen?
fields.placeholder: Pick the closest frequency

fields.label: Anything special about when it happens?
fields.placeholder: User role, machine, branch, config, test data, or timing conditions.

fields.label: Exact error message or warning
fields.placeholder: Paste exact messages if available.

fields.label: Logs, traces, or debugger clues
fields.placeholder: Paste log snippets, stack traces, or debugger observations.

fields.label: Do you have a guess about the cause?
fields.placeholder: If you have a theory, describe it. If not, say Unknown.

fields.label: Suggested files or layers to inspect
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely files when a feature is selected.

fields.label: How should we verify the fix worked?
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely checks when a feature is selected.

forms.title: Feature Logic Correction
forms.summary: Describe when the feature follows the wrong rules, path, or decision, using guided choices where possible.
whenToUse: Use this when the feature runs but behaves incorrectly according to user intent or business rules.
Use this when the UI may be fine but the workflow decisions are wrong.
Do not use this for styling-only requests.
beforeYouStart: Pick the module and feature first so the form can load helpful context.
Describe the intended workflow in plain language before describing the bug.
Think about which rule, branch, or condition is wrong.
endUserTips: Focus on intent versus actual behavior.
Use the guided choices first, then explain the mismatch in plain English.
Give examples of good and bad outcomes.
List what must keep working after the correction.

fields.label: Which workflow area is this in?
fields.placeholder: Pick the closest workflow area

fields.label: Which screen or step is involved?
fields.placeholder: Pick the main screen or step

fields.label: What kind of logic problem is this?
What is this feature supposed to do?
fields.placeholder: Describe the intended business behavior in plain language.

fields.label: What does it do today instead?
fields.placeholder: Describe the current behavior.

fields.label: Which rule or expectation is being broken?
fields.placeholder: State any rule or assumption the current logic breaks.

fields.label: Which conditions make this matter?
fields.placeholder: One condition per line

fields.label: What data or records affect this behavior?
fields.placeholder: Describe fields, stored procedures, or external data involved.

fields.label: Suggested layers or services involved
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely files or layers from the selected feature.

fields.label: What should stay the same while fixing this?
Any other constraints to preserve?
fields.placeholder: Add extra architecture, performance, or compatibility constraints if needed.

fields.label: What rules should the feature follow instead?
fields.placeholder: One rule per line

fields.label: Which scenarios should prove the fix?
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely success checks when a feature is selected.

fields.label: What must not break while fixing this?
fields.placeholder: List related flows that must remain stable.

forms.title: New Feature Request
forms.summary: Describe a new capability you want added, with guided picks that keep the request concrete and implementation-ready.
whenToUse: Use this when the capability does not exist yet and needs to be added.
Use this when the request spans new UI, workflow behavior, service logic, or supporting data flow.
Do not use this when the feature already exists and only needs a bug fix or small update.
beforeYouStart: Pick the closest module or feature first so the form can suggest nearby files and constraints.
Describe the user problem before describing the implementation idea.
Think about what success looks like for the user, not just the developer.
endUserTips: Focus on the new user capability in plain English.
Use the suggested files and constraints when they fit, but keep the request outcome-focused.
Be explicit about what is in scope versus what should wait.

fields.label: Short feature title
fields.placeholder: Add receiving exception review step

forms.title: Feature Removal Request
forms.summary: Describe a feature, screen, workflow step, or supporting path that should be removed or retired, with guardrails that keep the cleanup safe and explicit.
whenToUse: Use this when an existing capability should be removed, retired, or replaced.
Use this when the main request is cleanup of obsolete workflow, UI, logic, routing, or supporting files.
Do not use this when the feature should stay and only needs a bug fix or redesign.
beforeYouStart: Be clear about what exists today and why it should be removed.
List any user paths, data, or integrations that may still depend on it.
Decide whether this is a hard removal, soft retirement, or replacement.
endUserTips: State the removal reason in plain English before describing implementation cleanup.
Be explicit about what must disappear versus what must remain or be redirected.
If another workflow replaces this one, name that replacement clearly.

fields.label: Short removal title
fields.placeholder: Remove legacy receiving export shortcut

fields.label: Which workflow area is this closest to?
fields.placeholder: Pick the closest workflow area

fields.label: What kind of removal is this?
options: Hide from UI but keep backend support

fields.label: Why should this feature be removed or retired?
fields.placeholder: Describe why this capability is obsolete, harmful, duplicated, or no longer wanted.

fields.label: What should users do instead, if anything?
fields.placeholder: Name the replacement workflow, screen, or expected behavior if one exists.

fields.label: How broad can this removal be?
What user-visible pieces should be removed?
fields.placeholder: Screens, buttons, menu items, workflow steps, messages, and so on.

fields.label: Suggested files, services, or paths that may need cleanup
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely files from the selected feature.

fields.label: What may still depend on this feature?
fields.placeholder: Describe routes, commands, tests, docs, data flows, or integrations that may still reference it.

fields.label: Are there any data, settings, or migration concerns?
fields.placeholder: Describe saved settings, persisted records, feature flags, or migration notes if relevant.

fields.label: What must stay intact while removing this?
fields.placeholder: Name nearby workflows, screens, or architecture boundaries that must not be damaged.

fields.label: How will we know the removal is complete?
fields.placeholder: One acceptance check per line

fields.label: What validation or cleanup checks should happen?
fields.placeholder: Compile, tests, search checks, docs cleanup, manual checks, and so on.

forms.title: Performance Issue / Optimization
forms.summary: Describe slowness, lag, or resource-heavy behavior with enough structure to guide measurement, diagnosis, and safe optimization.
whenToUse: Use this when the main issue is slowness, lag, heavy resource usage, or poor responsiveness.
Use this for UI stalls, slow workflows, expensive queries, or repeated work that should be reduced.
Do not use this when the main problem is incorrect behavior rather than performance.
beforeYouStart: Pick the closest module or feature first so the form can suggest likely hotspots.
Describe where the slowdown is visible and how bad it feels.
Capture any timing, dataset size, or machine details that affect the issue.
endUserTips: State what feels slow in plain English before guessing why.
If you have timing numbers, include them. If not, describe the user impact.
Be clear about what acceptable performance would look like.

fields.label: Short performance issue title
fields.placeholder: Receiving history grid stalls on first load

fields.label: Which workflow area is this in?
fields.placeholder: Pick the closest workflow area

fields.label: Which screen or step feels slow?
fields.placeholder: Pick the main screen or step

fields.label: What kind of performance problem is this?
What is the user seeing today?
fields.placeholder: Describe the slowness, lag, or stall in plain English.

fields.label: What should acceptable performance look like?
fields.placeholder: Describe the expected responsiveness, timing, or user experience target.

fields.label: How often does this happen?
What timing numbers or measurements do you have?
fields.placeholder: Load times, stopwatch numbers, trace notes, or profiler clues.

fields.label: What data size or runtime context affects this?
fields.placeholder: Describe record counts, machine conditions, or environment differences.

fields.label: Suggested files, layers, or hotspots to inspect
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely files from the selected feature.

fields.label: What must stay true while optimizing this?
fields.placeholder: Mention behavior, architecture, or compatibility boundaries that must not change.

fields.label: How should we prove the performance work helped?
fields.placeholder: List timing checks, scenario re-tests, or measurement comparisons.

forms.title: Configuration / Environment Issue
forms.summary: Describe problems caused by configuration, machine setup, environment differences, or startup wiring with enough detail to diagnose safely.
whenToUse: Use this when the issue depends on config values, environment setup, appsettings, startup registration, or machine-specific behavior.
Use this when the workflow is fine in one environment but breaks in another.
Do not use this for pure business logic issues that are independent of environment or configuration.
beforeYouStart: State which environment or machine shows the problem.
Capture the exact error or mismatch if possible.
Think about what config source is expected to control this behavior.
endUserTips: Facts about environment differences matter more than guesses.
Mention whether this is local only, shared, or production-like.
Do not paste secrets; describe the setting source instead.

fields.placeholder: Receiving app fails to load module metadata locally

fields.label: Which workflow area is closest to the issue?
fields.placeholder: Pick the closest workflow area

options: VS Code task or local tooling

fields.label: What kind of config or setup seems involved?
What is happening today?
fields.placeholder: Describe the failure or mismatch exactly as observed.

fields.label: What should happen instead?
fields.placeholder: Describe the expected startup, load, or environment-dependent behavior.

fields.label: How can someone reproduce this?
fields.placeholder: One step per line

fields.label: Exact errors, warnings, or startup clues
fields.placeholder: Paste exact messages, stack traces, or console output if available.

fields.placeholder: List config files, task names, environment vars, or startup areas.

fields.placeholder: Mention secrets handling, environment safety, or compatibility limits.

fields.label: How should we prove the config or environment fix worked?
fields.placeholder: List startup checks, environment comparisons, or safe verification steps.

forms.title: Naming / Consistency Cleanup
forms.summary: Describe rename and consistency cleanup work where behavior should stay the same but names, labels, or structure should better match reality.
whenToUse: Use this when file, type, method, variable, or feature names no longer match what the code really does.
Use this for semantic cleanup, consistency fixes, and no-behavior-change renaming work.
Do not use this when the main request is to change business logic.
beforeYouStart: Describe what is misleading today and what the name should communicate instead.
List the main artifacts that need consistency cleanup.
Be explicit if this should avoid behavior changes.
endUserTips: State the naming problem in plain English.
Use concrete old-versus-new examples where possible.
Be clear about whether this includes code comments, docs, or UI labels too.

forms.title: UI Change + Logic Change
forms.summary: Describe requests where the screen and the behavior need to change together, so the UI update and rule changes stay aligned.
whenToUse: Use this when the workflow experience and the underlying rules both need to change.
Use this when a pure UI-only or pure logic-only form would split one request too artificially.
Do not use this when only one side needs work.
beforeYouStart: Describe the current user experience and the current rule mismatch separately.
Be clear about what the user should see and what the system should decide.
Think about what must remain stable while both layers change together.
endUserTips: Treat the visual behavior and the business behavior as two parts of one request.
Use the guided picks for both UI and logic concerns.
Make the acceptance criteria prove both the screen and the rules changed correctly.

forms.title: Debugging + Logging
forms.summary: Describe a bug that needs diagnosis plus logging improvements so the investigation and observability work stay together.
whenToUse: Use this when you need to debug a problem and adjust logging at the same time.
Use this when current logs are missing, noisy, or not useful enough to diagnose the issue safely.
Do not use this when the bug is clear and no logging changes are needed.
beforeYouStart: Capture the current symptom and any exact error messages first.
Think about what logging is missing or unhelpful today.
Be explicit about what must never be written to logs.
endUserTips: Lead with facts about the bug, then explain how logging should help.
If you only need temporary diagnostic logs, say that.
Use the verification section to prove both the fix and the logging quality.

forms.title: Logic Change + Test Generation

whenToUse: Use this when the main work is a logic fix and the request should include tests as part of the same change.

Do not use this when the request is only to add tests without changing behavior.

Think about the few test scenarios that best prove the rule change.

endUserTips: Treat the rule change and the test plan as one request.
Use plain English for the intended behavior first, then list the key scenarios.
Make sure the do-not-regress section covers nearby flows that should stay stable.

forms.title: Code Review Request
forms.summary: Describe what you want reviewed, with guided choices that focus the review on defects, risks, and missing validation.
whenToUse: Use this when you want Copilot to review a feature, module, or set of changes for problems.
Use this when the main goal is finding risks rather than implementing a requested change.
Use this for review of planned or completed work.
beforeYouStart: Pick the module and feature first so the form can suggest likely files and risks.
State whether this is a broad review or a focused review.
Think about the biggest risks you want reviewed first.
endUserTips: If the codebase area is large, narrow the review to the feature or files that matter most.
Say whether you want critical defects only or a fuller review.
Mention any recent changes or unstable areas the reviewer should keep in mind.

Capture the exact mismatch, bad result, or SQL error if you can.
endUserTips: Paste exact SQL or stored procedure names when available.
Use the suggested files or database objects if they match.
Mention constraints like read-only Visual access or stored-procedure-only rules.

forms.title: Test Generation Request
forms.summary: Describe which code needs tests and what those tests must prove, with guided file and scenario suggestions.
whenToUse: Use this when you want Copilot to add or expand tests for a file, class, or module.
Use this when the main output is tests, not production code changes.
Use this for unit or integration test requests.
beforeYouStart: Pick the module and feature first so the form can suggest likely files and scenarios.
Decide whether you want unit, integration, or mixed tests.
Think about the few behaviors that matter most.
endUserTips: State the test scope clearly.
Use the suggested files or scenarios if they match your need.
Focus on behaviors that matter more than a coverage number.

fieldGroups.title: Test Target
fieldGroups.description: Use the mirrored test structure under MTM_Receiving_Application.Tests/Unit. Existing unit tests live under the matching module and subfolder path, and new test files should be created there to mirror the production code location.

fields.label: Which workflow area is this in?
fields.placeholder: Pick the closest workflow area

fields.label: Short test request title
fields.placeholder: Add ViewModel tests for receiving workflow

fields.label: What kind of tests do you want?
Suggested files, classes, or modules to test
fields.placeholder: Example: Module_Receiving/ViewModels/ViewModel_Receiving_Workflow.cs -> MTM_Receiving_Application.Tests/Unit/Module_Receiving/ViewModels/ViewModel_Receiving_Workflow_Tests.cs
fields.helpText: Find or create unit tests under MTM_Receiving_Application.Tests/Unit using the same module and folder path as the application code. Mirror the source location, then add or edit the matching *_Tests.cs file there.

fields.label: What must these tests prove?
fields.placeholder: Describe core behaviors, edge cases, and failure paths.

fieldGroups.title: Coverage Details
fieldGroups.description: Call out whether the work belongs in Unit or Integration tests. In this repo, most mocked logic tests belong under MTM_Receiving_Application.Tests/Unit, while database-backed checks should stay out of unit test requests.

fields.label: Which dependencies should be mocked or kept real?
fields.placeholder: Describe services, DAOs, database access, or framework dependencies.

fields.label: Which scenarios absolutely need coverage?
fields.placeholder: The form can prefill likely scenarios from the selected feature.

fields.label: What testing rules or limits matter here?
fields.placeholder: Naming patterns, no database access, use FluentAssertions, create tests under MTM_Receiving_Application.Tests/Unit/<matching module path>, and so on.
fields.helpText: If you know the exact test file location, spell it out here. Example: create or edit MTM_Receiving_Application.Tests/Unit/Module_Core/Helpers/Helper_Name_Tests.cs to cover Module_Core/Helpers/Helper_Name.cs.
